mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series 530i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 530i engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only, with the N55 and B48 variants at the higher end. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £800–£2,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 5 Series.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 5 Series 530i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you have. The M54 (E39/early E60) is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable engines — with cooling system maintenance the only real concern. The N52 (E60 2006–2007) is reliable but has oil leak issues. The N53 (F10 2010–2013) suffers from injector and HPFP problems. The N55 (F10 2013–2017) is more robust. The B48 (G30 2017+) is the most refined and reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series 530i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M54 cooling system failures (E39/early E60) — plastic water pump and thermostat housing components fail. Oil leaks across all generations — particularly valve cover and oil filter housing. N53 injector and HPFP issues (F10 2010–2013). VANOS seal wear on the M54. The B48 (G30) is significantly improved.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 530i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the generation and vehicle condition. Against a well-kept G30 worth £15,000–£25,000, a £4,000–£5,500 recon is 15–35% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired E39 worth £2,000–£4,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. F10 values (£5,000–£12,000) make replacement borderline.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 5 Series 530i engine code?
Can I repair the M54 cooling system, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The cooling system components must be replaced — the water pump, thermostat housing, and radiator are all known failure points on the M54. A preventative overhaul at 60,000–80,000 miles costs £300–£800. Ignoring it can lead to sudden overheating and head gasket failure, which is far more expensive.

eraMap.title: The 530i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Rare 3.0L V8 — 218 hp; reliable and rugged; timing chain guides and oil leaks are the main watch items
3.0L straight-six; 231 hp; legendary reliability; cooling system is the critical weakness
Same M54 as E39 — smooth and reliable; cooling system remains the primary concern
3.0L N/A six; 258–272 hp; magnesium/aluminium block; oil leaks and eccentric shaft sensor are common
3.0L N/A six; 272 hp; direct injection; injector and HPFP issues are the main concerns
3.0L turbo six; 302 hp; single twin-scroll turbo; HPFP, VANOS and oil leaks are common
2.0L turbo four; 248–252 hp; timing chain issues resolved; robust and efficient

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 530i? Check These First
buyIf: * G30 (2017+) with the B48 engine (most reliable modern 530i)
* E39 or early E60 with cooling system recently overhauled
* F10 N55 (2013–2017) with documented service history (more reliable than N53)
* E34 V8 with documented maintenance history (rare and collectable)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (all generations)
avoidIf: * E39/E60 M54 with overheating history or unexplained coolant loss — cooling system may be compromised
* F10 N53 (2010–2013) with rough running or misfires — injector and HPFP issues likely
* Any 530i with no service history — all engines are sensitive to oil quality
* E34 V8 with no history — parts are increasingly scarce

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 530i is one of BMW's most significant badges — spanning the transition from V8s to straight-sixes to modern turbocharged four-cylinders. The E39 M54 is widely regarded as one of the most reliable BMW engines ever built, with cooling system maintenance the only real concern. The N53 F10 is the riskier modern choice — injector and HPFP issues are common. The G30 B48 is the most refined and reliable, with timing chain issues resolved. With typical values ranging from £4,000 (E39) to £25,000+ (G30), an engine replacement is a significant investment — the B48-engined G30 is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare 530i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 5 Series 530i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks, recent water pump/thermostat replacement (M54 critical) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for smooth idle; any rattles or rough running?
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover, oil filter housing, and oil pan for leaks
* Service History — Including which engine era and oil-change discipline
* VANOS Operation — Test low-end torque; any flat spot may indicate VANOS seal wear (M54)
* Injector Health — For N53 (F10 2010–2013): check for misfires or rough running
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate VANOS, injector or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
